what_it_does: Defines a single ROUTINE dictionary named self_status that configures a scheduled Claude Code session to report its own health in one line from /home/tim/company.
observations: The file is pure data: a docstring, a __future__ import, and one ROUTINE dict.
ROUTINE keys include id, label, description, prompt, cwd, permission_mode, cadence, repeats, max_turns, and trigger.
The prompt asks for exactly one short line stating the session is running, the current working directory, and MCP reachability.
cadence is OnCalendar=*-*-* 09:00:00, repeats is False, and max_turns is 1.
The docstring describes this as the canonical first routine and a smallest end-to-end proof that a routine fires through the supervisor.
The trigger field notes both a daily schedule arm and a manual op=fire path.
standouts: A data-only routine definition with no executable code beyond the dict literal.
Explicit cadence and permission metadata wrapped in a single module-level substrate.
Positioned in the docstring as the canonical first / proof-of-life routine.
conventions: Uses a module-level dict as the routine substrate.
Docstring tags the file with an S-R9.1 identifier and explains its role.
Keys mirror a routine schema: id, label, description, prompt, cwd, permission_mode, cadence, repeats, max_turns, trigger.
concerns: Hardcoded absolute path /home/tim/company may not be portable.
repeats False alongside a daily cadence is semantically unclear from this file alone.
permission_mode plan is not explained operationally here.
notes: A minimal, data-only routine definition that acts as a scheduled health-check prompt for the Company supervisor.
questions: What does permission_mode plan enforce at runtime?
How does repeats False interact with a daily OnCalendar cadence?
Is the hardcoded cwd intended to be environment-specific or should it be parameterized?
What is the meaning of the S-R9.1 identifier in the docstring?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to drive a real Claude Code session to report health in one line; the file actually contains only the static routine configuration dict, so it matches the stated purpose as a substrate but does not itself execute anything.
intention_signals: It is trying to establish a simple, observable proof that the routine runner can launch a session, deliver a prompt, and collect a one-line status reply.
novelty: Treats a scheduled health-check prompt as a first-class, versioned routine record with explicit cadence and permission metadata rather than an ad-hoc script.
contribution: Provides a concrete, minimal example of a routine record that a fused system can use to understand the expected schema and semantics of routine definitions (id, prompt, cwd, cadence, permission_mode, max_turns, repeats, trigger).
summary_for_embedding: routines/self_status.py defines the self_status routine, a data-only Python module containing a ROUTINE dictionary that schedules a one-line Claude Code health-check prompt at 09:00 daily from /home/tim/company.
intention_for_embedding: This file was intended to be the canonical minimal routine substrate for verifying that the Company routine runner can fire a session and capture a self-status reply.
